text: The surgeon constructs the S-pouch using three limbs of small bowel segments, each measuring twelve to fifteen centimeters. The segments are approximated with continuous 3-0 Vicryl sutures, taking seromuscular bites. The exit conduit, initially four to five centimeters long, is downsized to two centimeters to prevent long-term obstructive defecation. The surgeon begins the aneurotomy on the first limb, staying close to the suture line to prevent serosa puckering and ensure a greater pouch circumference.

The surgeon opens the middle lumen, providing an intraluminal view of the posterior wall. Closure is performed from proximal to distal using two sutures, which are tied in the center to ensure secure closure without relying on a single suture.

The surgeon uses a continuous 3-0 microbe suture on the next limb of the S-pouch, leaving two corner sutures in the proximal distal corners to aid in the creation of the anterior wall. The anterior wall is then sutured with running seromuscular bites, followed by reinforcement with interrupted thoracovacral sutures.

The surgeon downsizes the exit conduit to two centimeters, carefully assessing the blood supply and checking for arterial bleeding, which is crucial for anastomotic healing. An air leak test is conducted, confirming no leaks.
